speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde

words: There was Grandma Stuart waiting for them, and they were so delighted to see each other after the long trip. And Uncle Billy Taylor had come out and said, when are you folks just super good wagons? Right here and stay. I have garden and my trees were already coming into bearing, and we have milk and there's lots of water and you can just stay here while you go and look for homesteads.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And I've heard them say that some of them camped there for six months at the Taylor Place, while they looked for homesteads that they could settle on, and that was one of the big wagon trains into Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the these people settle near each other?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. The snow's in the clouds have joined fences ever since, and? And I always tell the snows we haven't quarreled about the fence line yet.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: But it certainly goes back many, many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The sow orchards came up and, and crops came up that fast that early?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. That fast. They came using 71 well and seven the fall of 77. Uncle Billy Taylor already had apple trees bearing, because I remember Grandma Clyde telling me she was a little girl when. But oh, how good the apples tasted. They'd come without any fresh things, and they were so delighted to get there, because the vegetables and the apples were so delicious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you heard about the first, the first homes that people built, like the the Kleins and.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: We they built. Yes. The first, the first they built a little a little log cabin. You know, they got here in September. Well, they went to the mountains and brought back trees and made a log cabin, and it had a dirt floor. And they lived in that the first year. And then the following spring they built the house.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And it's a house that we live in now. And they had the Hoke relatives and the settlers and they were good, for instance, they were good and doing left and plaster. This was the first house that was plastered in the country, but they no knew how to do it, and they were highly skilled at doing or making pottery.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They, they dug out a clay bank over here and built a brick hill, and they made the clay pottery crocks and things and the, the, the.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this for home.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Utensils? Yes, they were for home utensils. They made them for themselves. And we still have some of them around. But and Mr. Hoke was a skill of wheelwright, and they were skilled but many, many things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they had a little money when they came on?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think they did. They were, grandpa's employer was a veteran from the Civil War, and they had and he had been wounded and he had a government pension. Do you had a do a pension? He had had his some mouth shut away and he had a silver plate in his jaw. And so he drew a government pension.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: But all of them had a little money, but they had sold in Kansas because of the grasshopper playing in the drouth. And they were awfully glad to get into this country.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And they were Pennsylvania Dutch extraction. Grandma, is it there? And Grandma Clyde always wore the little bonnets of the Mennonites. You know. And they made all the old time, all the old time recipes, like for the red cabbage relish. And and you ought to make fritters. And they were very, very skilled in all the old Pennsylvania Dutch methods of cooking and doing things like they made their own butter.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They did all kinds of things like that. Beautiful quilters, most beautiful handwork, beautiful stitching. Did you ever want to see?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: An yes. It's like Paul said. Let the women be silent. All of them covered their heads, and it was almost a religion with them that you you were very modest and you kept your your neck up high, dresses always high at the neck and down around here, long sleeved and the bonnet over your head. It was part of the modesty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were so they wore dresses.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah. Know, along the side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean they wore the, you.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Know, the inside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As well as outside. There's some fellows coming out here and there's a fair amount of, what you call.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So. Well, these pioneer people carried the love of education with them. And almost the first thing they did when they got to this country was start schools. And there was a McGuire school started the very next spring after the wagon train 77 got in, when the first wagon train came in to Moscow in 71, the Haskell school was started that year, taught by Noel Whelan, who was a trained Baptist minister and a school teacher.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: The McGuire school was started shortly after that, which was out back of where the the Chinese Garden now is. There was some the love of education and the love of religion were innate in all these people. In 1870, about 1875, no. Lowell, established the Baptist church in what is now Moscow, at the very site for the present Baptist church now stands, the the county was soon divided into school districts, and they had land grants, sections of land, certain sections of land were set aside and they were called the school section.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And the profit from that land was to be used to support schools for our county. At one time we had 120 schools, and later our county for the times of consolidation and in the very, very early days, each school district was divided according to the walking distance of the children. Wouldn't have too far to walk to school, and the while the communities were very young, the teachers often boarded around from one house to another, and that would be part of their their pay in the very, very early days before the turn of the century, probably the school would just be held in the spring and then again in the fall when the weather would be

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: good. And of course, only the smaller children could attend school because the older ones had to help in the field and help in the house with the the numerous household duties that the small children had to go to school to learn to read and write. But this seemed to be one. One. This love of learning was something you need to all those pioneer, all those pioneer families.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I attended one of these rural schools in the early 1900s, and there'd be all grades from 1 to 8. The children would range in age from 6 to 21. Big ones and little ones all together, some of them older and bigger than our teachers and the teachers training. Most of them were rather mediocre any time they got enough education so they could pass the state examination, they could go out and become teachers with some of them.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They went through the eighth grade and then would take the examination and this would qualify them to teach. And none of the schools had many facilities. At first they were just big potbelly stoves, and the older boys would split. Some would light a fire to keep it warm. There were crude desks and seats and many of the early teachers were rather poorly trained, but some of them were good.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: The pay was very mediocre $40 a month and board of and board by boarding around, walking home, lots of the children had many miles to walk to get to school, and some of them rode horseback, and a little later they got eight month schools and these would run through the winter. And it was up to the parents to get the children down to school, or the children could ride horseback.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And most of the learning was the three hours reading, writing and arithmetic and very little else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the methods that they used to.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Pay out the big word? Help the little ones? That was one of the big advantages, because the older ones got a lesson in patience and how to deal with the little ones. So it wasn't all a lost cause. Often you could learn more from the older children than you did from the teacher, and when the teachers could sing, that was a great advantage because it brought a little music into the lives of the children.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: But with schools with 1450 children in them, it was learning was quite a hard job. I was fortunate in having some good teachers, and I like to read and learn and and I loved the library books. When we got to a place where they would bring in a few extra library books and enjoyed reading them. When I got to college, my teacher asked me if I'd ever read David Copperfield.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I said yes, I had that very 25 times. He was shocked. John Cushman was shocked that he said, well, what else did you do when you were in school? That that's just about all. I'd hurry through the lessons so I could go in and get the story books and read them, and I soon had them all read through, so then I'd have to go back and read them over again.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: But that was the means of escape from the mud and the other things that I didn't enjoy about the country schools, but there was some learning went on. At the same time, I think.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: We brought our own cold lunches, you know, generally packed in the tobacco box, then the old plug tobacco boxes, and by noon they were frozen. If you left them out in the halls, they were frozen nice and steep. Sometimes you'd bring them in and set them around the heater, you know, and keep them a little warm. And there are no hot lunches in those days.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So we didn't have that problem to contend with. And the thing I remember probably the best was how when I got to be a little older than I was allowed to go and help the younger ones, and I decided I wanted to be a teacher because it was rewarding to see them learn to read and be on their own a little bit.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So I think that was one of the things I got out of rural school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We have a have you recite?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Pretty much. Was that. Oh, yes. Every Friday afternoon we'd have recitations and we all learned little pieces. We'd get up and speak them. This was no hardship on me because I could learn them quickly and easily. And on Friday afternoon we'd entertain our parents of the parents could get out speaking pieces, and those who could sing would sing songs.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And the whole social life of each community centered around the school. It was one, one common bond of interest among the parents was the welfare of the children. It was always surprising how all the parents turned out for the Christmas programs and for the last day of school picnic, and it was the big social event of the year.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Some of the schools even went farther than that, but they had maybe Friday night parties and they played the all time party games. It was very considered very wrong in most of the schools to dance in those days. It was all right to play skipped Malu and happy is the Miller boy and the needles I and all the old times singing party games that were traditional to the early pioneer, settlements.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how how many of these games were played?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, yes. Nearly all of them had something to do with choosing partners, and this was very delightful for the young teenagers of that day. There wasn't any rock and roll, you know, but it was lots of fun choosing your partner for Miller Boy and and so on, and lots of the courting. And the early days went on at these play party games at the country schools and must have been very successful there.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I can look back and say many of the couples who met there and played there and walked home from there, all they're married and still sticking together after all these years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what you're saying is that the school was really the center of social life?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. That's right. They had baskets, suppers, you know, and the girls would fix up pretty baskets and sometimes they'd give a little hint of which one was theirs. If they had someone special, they wanted to buy it. And, and they all each of the girl divide to turn out the best cooking and the best cake and the nicest baskets.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And there was much bidding on the baskets and much rivalry among the young men to see who would get the right girls basket. And this was a meeting ground for the young people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this mostly just kids? Who did parents come along?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: The parents came too. They were generally well chaperoned by all the old folks of the community. It would be the social event of the week would be going down to the schoolhouse, to the Friday night Literary Society, or to the basket supper, to whatever the the school offered by way of entertainment. But it was their television and their movie house.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: It was everything that they had to break the monotony of the pioneer winters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about these these literary what, what kinds of.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Things did they belong there? Well, the literary society sometimes the all folks to to get up and have spirited debates, among themselves and sometimes the young and the young people would sing and then and then, we could play the violin. That was quite a, quite an attraction. And they would then there'd be recitations of the various grades and always, of course, community singing by everybody.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And it was an outlet. It was the social life of that community. And it wasn't all bad because there was very little drinking ever went on at any in them. And they were a nice place for the young folks to get together and meet. It was nice place for the children to show off a little bit, so the parents could enjoy what was going on, and the parents were always entertained, at least while their own children were up speaking.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: The parents were having a wonderful time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were some of the. Do you remember what they used to debate?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh my.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Days.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, mine. A lot of them had religious connotations like is the Bible true or is it not? And, of course, before that had been the questions of free silver and, debating about and, even in those days there were debates going on about the oil situation. Should we nationalize the oil? And that hasn't been settled after all these 70 years are still debating that, you know.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And resolved, that the Indians didn't get a fair shake. And that's another question still open for debate. So times haven't changed too much. You know, the same human human nature doesn't change much of the same. Human problems are still a subject for debate and for solving that, right? Yes.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And of course, the speaking of pieces, if you learned we had many memory jams that we were to learn, and if we learned them real well, then we'd get to speak them for the benefit of our fathers and mothers. And we learned many things. I can remember speaking about them, about and still sits the schoolhouse by the road.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And life is real. Life is earnest and the grave is not at school. And such little gems is as I had for the edification of the parents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These are poems.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: That's right. And if we could learn them, well then we got to show off a little bit because the last day of school was quite an occasion, doing a picnic, picnic dinner. And then we followed it with the recitations by the entire student body and the singing of songs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Day by day. Do you think most of the kids did look forward to going to school? Or do you think that they would just as soon live without it like they are today?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I think maybe it was mixed emotions. Mixed emotions.
